summary: Gruber uses the conceit of a Magic 8-Ball Q&A to dissect RealNetworks' Harmony software, which reverse-engineered Apple's FairPlay DRM to allow Real's music store purchases to play on iPods. He exposes the contradiction in Real CEO Rob Glaser's earlier plea for Apple to open the iPod while ignoring that iPods already play unprotected music. The piece frames Harmony as a desperate publicity stunt rather than a legitimate business move, noting that Real bypassed licensing negotiations entirely. Gruber highlights Apple's thinly veiled threat to break Harmony in future iPod updates and treats Real's talk of licensing the technology to others as laughable.
key_points: RealNetworks reverse-engineered Apple's FairPlay DRM without permission or licensing, translating Real's proprietary format into Apple's proprietary format
Rob Glaser's claim that the iPod only works with iTunes purchases was misleading, ignoring CD ripping and non-DRM files entirely
Real's true concern was its own declining format market share, not consumer choice on the iPod
Apple's press release was a clear signal it would deliberately break Harmony in future software updates
The whole Harmony initiative reads more as a publicity stunt than a viable business strategy
money_quotes: Isn't what he really means that it's the market share for RealNetworks' protected file format that will go down if it doesn't work on the iPod?

Do you think RealNetworks honestly expected Apple to allow them to unofficially support the iPod, without paying Apple a cent in licensing fees, after Apple refused to allow them to officially support it?
Doesn't this seem more like a desperate publicity stunt than a legitimate business strategy?
Apple's (market) share will go down if they continue to do this. The only way to presently put songs on an iPod is to (buy) them from iTunes.

We strongly caution Real and their customers that when we update our iPod software from time to time it is highly likely that Real's Harmony technology will cease to work with current and future iPods.
key_insight: RealNetworks' Harmony was a desperate attempt to piggyback on iPod dominance by reverse-engineering Apple's DRM, but it was transparently more about saving Real's own declining relevance than serving consumers.
